name: Round Table
description: The Round Table is a central artifact in Arthurian legend, symbolizing equality, unity, and chivalry. It was a large, circular table around which King Arthur and his knights would gather to discuss matters of the realm, plan quests, and celebrate victories. The table's round shape ensured that no one knight sat at its head, promoting the idea that all were equal in Arthur's court. According to some accounts, the Round Table was a wedding gift from Arthur's father-in-law, King Leodegrance, while others claim it was crafted by Merlin himself. It was said to accommodate 150 knights, each seat representing a knight's position in the fellowship. Empty seats, like the Siege Perilous, were reserved for those destined for great deeds, and attempting to sit in such a seat without being worthy would result in dire consequences. The Round Table served as a symbol of Arthur's enlightened rule and the ideals of chivalry, inspiring courage, loyalty, and justice among his knights. Its presence fostered a sense of camaraderie and shared purpose, enabling the Knights of the Round Table to undertake legendary quests and defend the realm of Camelot. The table's eventual dissolution mirrors the decline of Arthur's kingdom, marking the end of an era of chivalry and the triumph of darker forces.
subtitle: Equality and Chivalry Embodied
origin: Gift from King Leodegrance or crafted by Merlin
currentLocation: Unknown, possibly Avalon or lost to time

keyMyths.title: The Siege Perilous
keyMyths.description: The Siege Perilous was a seat at the Round Table reserved for the knight destined to achieve the Holy Grail. Only the purest knight could sit there without being consumed by flames or suffering some other terrible fate.
keyMyths.source: Le Morte d'Arthur by Sir Thomas Malory

keyMyths.title: The Round Table's Creation
keyMyths.description: The origins of the Round Table vary, with some accounts stating it was a wedding gift, while others attribute its creation to Merlin's magic. Regardless of its origin, it symbolized Arthur's commitment to equality and justice.

sources.author: Sir Thomas Malory
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Arthurian legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive compilation of Arthurian legends.

sources.author: Geoffrey of Monmouth
sources.description: An early account of King Arthur and his reign.
sources.text: An early account of King Arthur and his reign.

sources.author: Chrétien de Troyes
sources.description: A collection of Arthurian romances focusing on individual knights.
sources.text: A collection of Arthurian romances focusing on individual knights.

worshipPractices.name: Reverence for Chivalry
worshipPractices.description: While not direct worship, the ideals embodied by the Round Table, such as chivalry, honor, and justice, are deeply revered. Knights strive to emulate these virtues in their actions and decisions, seeing them as a path to moral excellence. The Table itself is a constant reminder of these principles.

worshipPractices.name: Oaths of Allegiance
worshipPractices.description: Knights swear oaths of allegiance to King Arthur and the ideals of the Round Table. These oaths are sacred vows, binding them to uphold justice, protect the innocent, and serve the realm with unwavering loyalty. Breaking an oath is considered a grave offense with serious consequences.

festivals.name: The Feast of Pentecost
festivals.description: A grand celebration is held at Camelot, marked by feasting, tournaments, and the initiation of new knights into the Round Table. Arthur uses the occasion to address the kingdom, reaffirm the values of the Table, and set forth new quests.

festivals.name: Victorious Returns
festivals.timing: Upon the return of successful quests
festivals.description: When knights return from quests, especially those of great importance, a feast is held at the Round Table to celebrate their achievements. Bards sing tales of their bravery, and they share their experiences with the assembled court, reinforcing the values and legacy of the Round Table.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Round Table continues to inspire modern concepts of leadership, teamwork, and ethical conduct. It's a symbol of egalitarian collaboration and the pursuit of noble ideals, frequently referenced in literature, film, and organizational structures to represent fairness and shared responsibility. The idea of a 'round table' discussion is still used to promote equal input.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Valhalla, Odin's hall, served as a gathering place for fallen heroes, similar to the Round Table. It fostered camaraderie and celebrated warriors, though focused on afterlife preparation.
Mount Olympus was the home of the Olympian gods, who would convene to discuss matters of importance. While not a physical table, the council symbolized a unified governing body.
Indra's Durbar (royal court) served as a meeting place for gods and celestial beings. It symbolizes a space for important decisions and royal counsel, mirroring the function of the Round Table.

associations.name: Equality
associations.significance: The round shape of the table ensured no one knight was positioned as superior, symbolizing equality among all members.

associations.name: Twelve
associations.significance: The Round Table is often associated with the Twelve Apostles, representing a sacred brotherhood and shared purpose.

associations.name: Chivalry
associations.significance: The Round Table embodies the ideals of chivalry, promoting honor, courage, and service to others.

associations.name: Fellowship
associations.significance: Knights of the Round Table were bound by loyalty and camaraderie, fostering a sense of fellowship and mutual support.

associations.name: Stone
associations.significance: The table's enduring presence represents the solid foundation of Arthur's kingdom and the unwavering commitment of his knights.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Gold represents the value and preciousness of the virtues and ideals associated with the Round Table.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: The sun's circular form mirrors the table's shape, symbolizing enlightenment, truth, and the radiant glory of Arthur's court.

companions.name: Merlin
companions.description: Merlin, the powerful sorcerer, acted as Arthur's advisor and guide. He used his magic and wisdom to aid Arthur in establishing his kingdom and assembling the Knights of the Round Table.

companions.name: Guinevere
companions.description: Guinevere, Arthur's queen, was a figure of grace and influence at Camelot. Her presence often provided counsel and support to Arthur and the Knights.

companions.name: Lancelot
companions.description: Sir Lancelot was one of the most valiant and skilled knights of the Round Table. He was a close friend and loyal companion to Arthur, though their relationship was complicated by his love for Guinevere.

companions.name: Excalibur
companions.description: Excalibur is Arthur's legendary sword, symbolizing his right to rule. It represents great power and authority, and is essential to Arthur's success as king.

companions.name: Galahad
companions.description: Sir Galahad, the purest knight, eventually achieves the quest for the Holy Grail. His presence represents spiritual perfection and divine favor within the Round Table's fellowship.

extendedContent.title: Creation & Forging
extendedContent.content:
The origins of the Round Table are shrouded in various tales across Arthurian literature. One prominent account attributes its creation to Merlin, the powerful wizard and advisor to King Arthur. In this version, Merlin designed the table to accommodate all of Arthur's knights without assigning precedence to any single individual, thereby fostering equality and preventing disputes over seating order. Another, perhaps earlier, tradition suggests that the Round Table was a wedding gift from Arthur's father-in-law, King Leodegrance of Cameliard, along with one hundred knights. Arthur then filled the remaining seats with his own chosen warriors. 

Geoffrey of Monmouth's *Historia Regum Britanniae* (c. 1136) doesn't explicitly mention a round table, but it does describe Arthur establishing a court of unparalleled chivalry. Later Romances, particularly those of Chrétien de Troyes and the Vulgate Cycle, elaborate on the Round Table's significance, emphasizing its role in fostering a community of knights dedicated to justice and virtuous conduct. The precise material of the table and the methods used in its forging are rarely specified, leaving much to the imagination of storytellers and artists.

Regardless of its precise origin, the Round Table's creation marks a crucial moment in the Arthurian narrative, signifying the establishment of a new order based on principles of equality and fellowship among knights. It represents a conscious effort to break away from traditional hierarchies and foster a sense of shared purpose in Arthur's kingdom.

extendedContent.title: Powers & Properties
extendedContent.content:
While the Round Table itself is not typically imbued with overt magical powers in the same way as Excalibur, it possesses unique properties that contribute to its legendary status. Its circular shape is paramount, symbolizing equality and the absence of hierarchy. This design inherently prevents any knight from claiming a position of superiority, reinforcing the ideal of a brotherhood where all members are valued equally. The presence of empty seats, notably the Siege Perilous, adds another layer of complexity. 

The Siege Perilous was reserved for the knight destined to achieve the Holy Grail. Legend held that only a truly pure and worthy knight could occupy this seat without being consumed by fire or suffering some other terrible fate. This seat served as a constant reminder of the knights' quest for spiritual perfection and the ultimate goal of finding the Holy Grail. Furthermore, some accounts suggest the Round Table possessed the ability to magically expand or contract to accommodate the appropriate number of knights, ensuring that all deserving individuals could participate in its meetings. 

Beyond its physical attributes, the Round Table holds a powerful symbolic property: its ability to inspire loyalty, camaraderie, and a shared commitment to justice. It serves as a focal point for the knights' oaths and vows, solidifying their dedication to Arthur and his ideals. The table's very existence promotes a culture of chivalry and encourages the knights to uphold the highest standards of conduct both in and out of battle.

extendedContent.title: Notable Occupants
extendedContent.content:
The Round Table was not merely a gathering place; it was populated by a cast of legendary knights, each contributing to the Arthurian tapestry. King Arthur himself occupied a central position, guiding his knights and embodying the ideals of kingship. Sir Lancelot, renowned for his unparalleled skill in combat and his tragic love for Queen Guinevere, was perhaps the most celebrated knight, though his flaws ultimately contributed to the downfall of Camelot. Sir Gawaine, known for his courtesy, loyalty, and unwavering commitment to chivalry, represented another pillar of Arthur's court.

Sir Galahad, the son of Lancelot and Elaine, was destined to occupy the Siege Perilous and achieve the Holy Grail. His purity and spiritual strength set him apart from the other knights and marked him as a chosen one. Sir Percival, initially a naive and unrefined youth, underwent a transformative journey in his quest for the Grail, ultimately demonstrating the power of faith and perseverance. Sir Tristan, famed for his tragic love affair with Isolde, exemplified the complexities of human desire and the potential for conflict between personal passions and knightly duty.

Other notable occupants included Sir Kay, Arthur's foster brother and seneschal, Sir Bedivere, the last of the knights to remain with Arthur after the final battle, and Sir Gareth, known for his humility and courage. Each knight brought unique strengths and weaknesses to the Round Table, creating a dynamic and multifaceted community that shaped the Arthurian world.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism
extendedContent.content:
The Round Table is saturated with rich symbolism, representing core values of Arthurian society. The most prominent symbol is equality. Its circular form, devoid of a head or foot, ensures that no knight holds a position of inherent superiority. This fosters a sense of camaraderie and mutual respect among Arthur's warriors, reinforcing the idea that all members are valued equally for their contributions to the realm. The table also symbolizes unity, bringing together knights from diverse backgrounds and regions under a common purpose: to uphold justice, defend the weak, and serve the king.

Chivalry is another key concept embodied by the Round Table. The knights who gathered around it pledged to adhere to a strict code of conduct, emphasizing honor, courage, courtesy, and loyalty. The Round Table served as a constant reminder of these vows, encouraging the knights to strive for moral excellence in all their actions. The quest for the Holy Grail, often associated with the Round Table, represents a spiritual journey towards enlightenment and redemption. The Siege Perilous symbolizes the ultimate test of worthiness, reserved for the knight who is pure enough to achieve the Grail.

Furthermore, the Round Table can be interpreted as a microcosm of Arthur's kingdom, reflecting the ideals and challenges of his reign. Its successes and failures mirror the rise and fall of Camelot, highlighting the importance of leadership, unity, and adherence to ethical principles. The Round Table's enduring presence in Arthurian legend underscores its timeless relevance as a symbol of equality, unity, chivalry, and the pursuit of higher ideals.

shortDescription: King Arthur's famed table, symbolizing equality among knights. Its round shape ensured no one held precedence, fostering unity and chivalry in Camelot.

wielders.name: King Arthur
wielders.context: Founder and leader of the Knights of the Round Table.

wielders.name: Merlin
wielders.context: Enchanter; advisor to Arthur who may have conceived the table.

wielders.name: Sir Lancelot
wielders.context: One of the most esteemed knights who sat at the table.
